Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic despite putting in decent hours. I'd have these intense bursts where I'd study for hours reading through materials and taking detailed notes, but then I'd disappear for days. I preferred working late at night when my anxiety felt more manageable, surrounded by my notebooks and printed PDFs. The routine existed more in my head than in any visible pattern.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly, though not always because of deadlines. Sometimes my anxiety would spike and I couldn't bring myself to engage with anything course-related for days. Other times I'd feel this surge of motivation and study intensively. Deadlines mattered less to me than my emotional state on any given day, which probably explains my terrible attendance despite my grades.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I absolutely needed to check an assignment deadline or submit something. Sometimes I'd log in just to download materials and then immediately log out to work offline. The LMS interface actually made my anxiety worse somehow, so I avoided lingering there. I'd get what I needed and leave.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got into a comfortable rhythm with the LMS itself. I made the discussion posts because I knew they mattered, but it always felt forced. The closest thing to routine was my habit of checking in briefly at the start of each week to see what was new, then retreating to study on my own terms.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were my lifeline since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and annotate them extensively in my own system. Discussion posts were surprisingly helpful too because writing out my thoughts helped me process concepts, even when interacting with others felt draining.
Q6: I barely watched the videos, maybe skimmed through them at most. Sitting through video content makes me restless and anxious, and I can read the same information much faster. I also ignored a lot of the optional interactive elements because they felt like busywork when I could be studying more efficiently on my own.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to understanding the material or improving my assignments. I needed to see the purpose immediately. If something felt like engagement for engagement's sake, I'd skip it. My time and emotional energy are limited, so I had to be strategic about where I invested them.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps, honestly. They'd sit there making me feel guilty for not doing them, adding to my anxiety. Required activities at least had clear boundaries—do this, submit it, done. With optional stuff, I never knew if I was doing enough, which just fed into my spiraling thoughts about whether I was really learning properly.
Q9: Almost all my real studying happened outside the LMS. I'd print readings, make handwritten notes, create my own summaries in Word documents. I used external academic databases and supplementary textbooks constantly. My desk was covered in annotated papers and color-coded notes that represented hours of work nobody could see.
Q10: Most of my learning happened this way, actually. I'd spend entire evenings working through concepts with my notes spread around me, re-reading and rewriting until things clicked. I'd talk through ideas alone in my room, test myself with questions I wrote. The LMS would show I downloaded something once, but not the twenty times I reviewed it offline.
Q11: Constantly. I'd download a reading, and that single download represented potentially weeks of engagement with that material. I'd return to the same PDF over and over, adding new annotations, connecting it to other concepts. One login, one download, but hours and hours of actual study time that remained completely invisible.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where everything felt impossible. My anxiety would convince me I was failing even when I wasn't, and I'd avoid the course entirely because engaging meant confronting those fears. The mindfulness exercises helped sometimes, but other times the sadness just won and I'd retreat into myself for days.
Q13: My anxiety was the biggest factor, more than workload or confusion. When I felt overwhelmed, the LMS became this threatening space I needed to avoid. Fatigue from poor sleep during anxious periods made everything harder. I'd sometimes have the energy to study from my notes but not enough resilience to face the online platform itself.
Q14: Yes, sometimes I'd scroll through materials in a fog, not really absorbing anything. My eyes would move across the screen but my mind was elsewhere, consumed by worry. I'd realize I'd spent twenty minutes on a page and retained nothing. Those sessions felt like going through motions to quiet the voice telling me I wasn't trying hard enough.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially anything requiring real-time interaction or attendance. The anxiety made it feel safer to delay, even when I knew procrastination would make things worse. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt stronger, but sometimes tomorrow never came. It's a painful cycle I'm very aware of.
Q16: Deadlines created these pressure points where I'd have to overcome my avoidance. I'd often work intensively right before due dates, producing good work but at great emotional cost. The deadline forced engagement that my anxiety otherwise prevented. Without them, I might have drifted even more than I already did.
Q17: Good feedback gave me temporary relief from the constant worry that I was failing. It validated that my invisible studying was actually working. But the effect never lasted—within days, the anxiety would return, convincing me the next assignment would expose me as incompetent. Feedback helped momentarily but couldn't fix the underlying fear.
Q18: The flexible structure was both a blessing and a curse. It allowed me to work around my anxiety and study in my own way, which helped my performance. But the lack of forced attendance meant I could avoid too easily, feeding my disengagement from the actual class community. More structure might have helped me feel less adrift.
Q19: Having materials available for download was essential—without that, I couldn't have succeeded. The discussion format worked for me since I could compose thoughts carefully in writing. But the video-heavy content was hard, and the lack of clear weekly checkpoints made it easy to lose track of where I should be.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—anxious, searching for reassurance, checking things repeatedly without being able to commit to submission. Or they might be genuinely trying to engage but struggling to translate activity into completed work. High activity can mask paralysis as easily as it can show dedication.
Q21: That's essentially my story. My exam scores and assignment grades don't match my attendance or login frequency at all. I did the learning, just not in visible ways. Some students are effective at independent study and don't need constant platform engagement to succeed. The LMS isn't the only place learning happens.
Q22: Logs can show patterns of access, timing, and completion rates. They're good at tracking what you clicked and when. But they miss depth of engagement, emotional state, offline work, and the quality of learning. They see the shadow of engagement, not the substance. For someone like me, they'd show almost nothing of my actual effort.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, thinking about it even when you're not studying, caring about understanding rather than just passing. It's an internal state more than an external behavior. I was deeply engaged with the content intellectually, even while being disengaged from the course community and platform.
Q24: The LMS would miss my extensive offline study, my genuine intellectual investment in the material, and the constant battle with anxiety that shaped every interaction I had with the course. It wouldn't show how hard I worked to produce those assignments, or how much the sadness and fear cost me even when I succeeded. My grades look easy; they weren't.
